ntent3</div><div id="ctl00_PlaceHolderMain_rNotes__ControlWrapper_RichHtmlField" class="ms-rtestate-field" style="display:inline" aria-labelledby="ctl00_PlaceHolderMain_rNotes_label"><h3>Advice about Tariff Concession Orders</h3><p>Tariff Concession Orders (TCO) are an Australian Government revenue concession that exists where there are no known Australian manufacturers of goods that are substitutable for imported goods.</p><p>In order to claim a TCO in accordance with Australian law, your imported goods must: </p> <ul><li>firstly be classifiable to the tariff classification to which the TCO is keyed to

--- near 'concession' ---
ny commonly used words that describe your goods as possible into your search, as it is possible that a number of different TCOs may apply for those goods. olderMain_rNotes_label" style='display:none'>PublishingPageContent3</div><div id="ctl00_PlaceHolderMain_rNotes__ControlWrapper_RichHtmlField" class="ms-rtestate-field" style="display:inline" aria-labelledby="ctl00_PlaceHolderMain_rNotes_label"><h3>Advice about Tariff Concession Orders</h3><p>Tariff Concession Orders (TCO) are an Australian Government revenue concession that exists where there are no known Australian manufacturers of goods that are substitutable for imported goods.</p><p>In order to claim a TCO in accordance with Australian law, your imported goods must: </p> <ul><li>firstly be classifia

--- near 'Concession' ---
describe your goods as possible into your search, as it is possible that a number of different TCOs may apply for those goods. You will also need to ensure that your goods meet the tariff classification listed in the TCO.</p><p> If you import goods, and claim a revenue concession through a Tariff Concession Order, you need to be aware that penalties may apply if your goods do not precisely match the TCO description and/or its tariff classification. </p><p> The onus is on an importer/broker to correctly enter the goods, including declaring which particular TCO applies.
